coredata.dc:title: d-arginine-functionalized carbon dots with enhanced near-infrared emission and prolonged metabolism time for tumor fluorescent-guided photothermal therapy
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Liming
Wu, Jun
Wang, Bingzhe
Xing, Guichuan
Qu, Songnan
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have garnered significant interest owing to their distinctive optical properties. However, their bioimaging and biomedical applications are limited by pronounced fluorescence (FL) quenching in aqueous media and low tumor accumulation efficacy associated with their ultra-small size. This study proposes a simple surface modification approach using functioning d-arginine on CDs (d-Arg@CDs) to improve their near-infrared (NIR) FL in aqueous solution and maintain their high photothermal conversion properties. Because of the low utilization rate of dextral amino acids in animals, modifying CDs with low molecular weight d-arginine did not increase particle size but extended the metabolism time in blood circulation, thereby leading to enhanced accumulation efficacy at tumor sites in the mice model. The enhanced tumor accumulation of d-Arg@CDs resulted in significantly superior tumor NIR FL imaging and photothermal therapy performance compared with pure CDs and l-arginine functionalized CDs. This dextral amino acid modification approach is expected to be an effective tool for enhancing the biomedical applications of CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: d-arginine
Carbon dots
Near-infrared-emitting
Tumor penetration
Photothermal therapy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic of the preparation process of d-Arg@CDs. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra. (c) Fluorescence (FL) spectra under excitation at 550 nm. (d) Fluorescence spectra under excitation at 630 nm. (e) Photoluminescence (PL) decay curves recorded at 650 nm under 512 nm excitation. (f) Peak positions of PL and temperature evolutions derived from spectra (c), (d), and (g). (g) Temperature evolutions of CDs and d-Arg@CDs aqueous solutions with different weight ratios of CDs and d-arginine.
TEM images of (a) CDs and (b) d-Arg@CDs; (c) FT-IR spectra of d-Arg@CDs, CDs, and d-Arg; (d) 1H NMR spectra of CDs, d-Arg, and d-Arg@CDs in D2O.

Excitation-emission maps of (a) CDs and (b) d-Arg@CDs in aqueous solutions. TA spectra of (c) CDs and (d) d-Arg@CDs in aqueous solution at indicated delay times after excitation at λpump = 400 nm (1 kHz, 50 fs) with an excitation density of 5.0 μJ cm−2. Bleach signal kinetics of the (e) CDs and (f) d-Arg@CDs aqueous solution for λpump = 400 nm and probe wavelengths of 550 nm (red symbols/lines) and 630 nm (blue symbols/lines). The dots represent experimental points, and the solid lines represent fitting lines. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Assays of CDs and d-Arg@CDs 100 μg/mL showing relative viability of HepG2, Huh7, and Hepa1-6 cells for 24 h (n = 4 for each group); (b) Zeta potential spectra of CDs and d-Arg@CDs in aqueous solutions (c) FL confocal microscopy images of Hepa1-6 cells incubated with CDs and d-Arg@CDs for 1.5 h under excitation at 405 nm and 543 nm. (d) Live/death cell imaging assays of Hepa1-6 cells treated with d-Arg@CDs and CDs at a concentration of 100 μg/mL before and after 655 nm laser irradiation. Data are presented as mean ± SD.

(a) Near-infrared (NIR) FL images of mice injected with CDs, l-Arg@CDs, and d-Arg@CDs for the indicated times. (b) NIR FL images of mouse bodies after intravenous injection of CDs, l-Arg@CDs, and d-Arg@CDs at various time points.

(a) Infrared thermal images of mice intravenously injected with PBS, CDs, and d-Arg@CDs under 655-nm laser irradiation during the indicated periods. (b) Photographs of mice on corresponding days after different treatments. (c) Tumor growth curves and representative tumors on day 14 (n = 4 for each group) calculated based on mean tumor size for each mouse. Representative excised tumor tissues. (insets) (d) Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E), cleaved caspase 3 (c-Cas-3), and Ki67 staining of tumor tissues 14 days after treatment in mice. Data are presented as mean ± SD.

head.ce:title: d-arginine-functionalized carbon dots with enhanced near-infrared emission and prolonged metabolism time for tumor fluorescent-guided photothermal therapy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Joint Key Laboratory of the Ministry of Education
Institute of Applied Physics and Materials Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study demonstrates the successful fabrication of d-Arg@CDs to enhance near-infrared (NIR) fluorescence in aqueous solutions while maintaining exceptional photothermal conversion properties. The study also reveals that modifying CDs with d-arginine extends blood circulation, leading to enhanced accumulation at tumor sites in mice, without increasing particle size. By leveraging these findings, the researchers successfully accomplished tumor fluorescent-guided photothermal therapy.

Carbon dots (CDs) have garnered significant interest owing to their distinctive optical properties. However, their bioimaging and biomedical applications are limited by pronounced fluorescence (FL) quenching in aqueous media and low tumor accumulation efficacy associated with their ultra-small size. This study proposes a simple surface modification approach using functioning d-arginine on CDs (d-Arg@CDs) to improve their near-infrared (NIR) FL in aqueous solution and maintain their high photothermal conversion properties. Because of the low utilization rate of dextral amino acids in animals, modifying CDs with low molecular weight d-arginine did not increase particle size but extended the metabolism time in blood circulation, thereby leading to enhanced accumulation efficacy at tumor sites in the mice model. The enhanced tumor accumulation of d-Arg@CDs resulted in significantly superior tumor NIR FL imaging and photothermal therapy performance compared with pure CDs and l-arginine functionalized CDs. This dextral amino acid modification approach is expected to be an effective tool for enhancing the biomedical applications of CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:sections.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a novel ultra-small carbon nanomaterial, offer notable advantages, such as low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, tunable bandgap emissions, and versatile photophysical properties [1–5]. Recently, CD-based biomedical applications have garnered considerable interest, [6,7] particularly in fluorescence (FL) imaging [8–10] and tumor therapies [11–14]. Employing near-infrared (NIR) luminescent CDs offers advantages such as improved tissue imaging capabilities and enhanced penetration depth. However, efficient NIR luminescence in CDs remains a significant challenge. Among the various tumor therapy approaches, tumor photothermal therapy (PTT) stands out for its minimal side effects and distinct advantages [15–19]. Nevertheless, achieving efficient NIR emission and high photothermal conversion efficiency poses a substantial challenge because of the competing photophysical processes of FL (radiation transition) and PTT (non-radiation transition) [20]. Consequently, achieving a balance between FL and photothermal processes in CDs can expand their biomedical applications.

A major challenge is the limited NIR efficiency exhibited by CDs in aqueous solutions, which limits their effectiveness in FL imaging [21–23]. Overcoming the quenching effect of CDs in aqueous solutions and improving their NIR luminescence have emerged as critical objectives [11,24]. To address this challenge, various approaches have been proposed, such as surface passivation, [25–28] energy transfer processes, [29] and structural modifications [30–32]. However, simultaneously achieving efficient NIR luminescence and effective tumor PTT with CDs remains a challenge. Meanwhile, improving the enrichment efficiency of CDs in tumors typically requires increasing their particle size, which enhances tumor targeting but raises concerns about in vivo enrichment [20]. Therefore, the primary challenge is to achieve simultaneous enhancements in NIR FL and PTT without resorting to increasing the particle size.

In our previous study, CDs were successfully synthesized to exhibit NIR luminescence and high photothermal functionality conversion efficiency [16,33]. Nevertheless, these CDs experience FL quenching in aqueous environments, and their small size limits effective tumor enrichment, thereby affecting the efficacy of photothermal therapy. In this study, these challenges were addressed through the introduction of d-Arg modification, which not only improved the NIR luminescence of CDs but also extended their metabolism time in vivo, resulting in enhanced tumor enrichment and substantially improved photothermal treatment efficacy.

The synthesis of CDs followed the methodology outlined in our previous study. d-Arg and CD complexes were prepared by mixing the CDs (1 mg mL−1) with varying concentrations of d-arginine (ranging from 1 mg mL−1 to 100 mg mL−1) in an aqueous solution at 45℃ for 15 min, followed by cooling to room temperature, as illustrated in Fig. 1 a. Excited electrons are typically released to the ground state through radiation or non-radiation transitions in the form of heat. To balance the FL and photothermal processes of CDs after complexing with d-arginine, the optical and photothermal conversion properties of CDs and d-arginine complexes were evaluated at various mass ratios to optimize the complexing ratio. The UV–vis absorption band at 665 nm from the CDs in the complexes first improved and slightly blue-shifted at mass ratios less than 1:10, and then gradually decreased as the concentration of d-arginine increased 10 times higher than that of the CDs, as shown in Fig. 1b and Fig. S1. Under 550 and 630 nm excitations, the FL emission bands peaked at 650 and 700 nm, respectively, from the CDs and d-arginine complexes substantially increased by increasing the d-arginine concentration to 10 times that of the CDs. Subsequently, the rate of enhancement gradually decreased with further increases in the d-arginine concentration (Fig. 1c, d, Fig. S2 and S3). The PL decay curves of the red emissions from the CDs and d-arginine complexes exhibited longer lifetimes than those of the pure CDs (Fig. 1e), which agreed well with the enhanced emission spectra. A 655-nm laser with a power intensity of 1 W/cm2 was used to comparatively examine the photothermal performance of the CDs, CDs (100 μg/mL) and d-arginine complexes in aqueous solution. The pure CDs demonstrated remarkable photothermal performance, with their 100 μg/mL aqueous solution reaching 73.5℃ from room temperature after 10 min of 655-nm laser irradiation. After complexing with d-arginine at mass ratios up to 10 times that of CDs, the photothermal performance of the complexes slightly decreased but maintained a high-temperature increase of approximately 70℃ under the same laser irradiation. However, at higher d-arginine concentrations, this value decreased to 45.8℃, indicating a decrease in photothermal performance at high d-arginine complexation concentrations. Interestingly, the NIR luminescence (Em 700 nm) was maximally enhanced at a complexing mass ratio of 1:10 (Fig. 1f). Considering the significantly improved NIR emission and satisfactory photothermal performance, a mass ratio of 1:10 was selected as the optimal complexing condition, and the CDs and d-arginine complexes at this ratio were named d-Arg@CDs. Through calculation, it was found that the photothermal conversion efficiency of d-Arg@CDs aqueous solution was as high as 55 %. CDs had a low PLQY of 0.2 % in NIR region under 655-nm excitation in aqueous solution. After complexing with d-arginine, d-Arg@CDs showed enhanced PLQY of 1.1 % in NIR region under 655-nm excitation.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to characterize the morphologies of CDs and d-Arg@CDs, as illustrated in Fig. 2 a and b. The TEM images reveal that CDs and d-Arg@CDs were uniformly dispersed with similar diameters of approximately 6 nm, indicating that the d-arginine modification on the CDs did not increase the particle size. High-resolution TEM images of the CDs and d-Arg@CDs revealed well-defined uniform lattice fringes of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) lattice plane of graphite. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was used to verify the surface-bound interactions between d-arginine and CDs. As shown in Fig. 2c, a broad band at 3360 cm−1 corresponds to the ν(NH) stretching vibration of the guanidino group in d-arginine. The formation of hydrogen bonds between the guanidine group of d-arginine and the carboxyl group of CDs causes changes in the NH stretching vibration of the guanidine group, resulting in a noticeable shift in the peak position. The peak observed at 1699 cm−1 in the infrared spectrum of CDs corresponds to the −CO. A noticeable shift in peak position was observed upon the formation of hydrogen bonds via complexation with d-arginine. As shown in Fig. 2d, the complexation was further reflected in the chemical position changes observed in the 1H NMR spectra of CDs, d-arginine, and d-Arg@CDs. The amino group in d-arginine exhibited a chemical shift at 3.14 ppm. However, the amino group signal shifted to 3.34 ppm upon combining CDs with d-arginine. This phenomenon is attributed to the formation of hydrogen bonds between the –NH2 group of d-arginine and hydrogen bond receptors (–COOH/CO) on CDs, resulting in changes in the chemical shift values.

To examine the alterations in luminescent properties after modification with d-arginine, the excitation (EX)–emission (EM) maps of d-Arg@CDs and CDs in aqueous solutions were comparatively analyzed, as illustrated in Fig. 3 a and b. CDs exhibit a distinct green emission center at 550 nm, corresponding to the core state. In contrast, d-Arg@CDs demonstrated an improved NIR emission center at approximately 700 nm, which can be attributed to the surface-related state. To further examine the photodynamic processes of CDs and d-Arg@CDs, transient absorption spectroscopy measurements were performed under 400-nm excitation. Upon photoexcitation, a broad ground-state bleach signal corresponding to the carbon core emerges around 570 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 3c–f. Conversely, the d-arginine modification resulted in a pronounced surface-related state bleaching signal around 650 nm, indicating an energy transfer process from the core. The kinetics of the bleaching signal revealed an energy transfer rate of 4.1 × 1011 s−1. These results indicate that d-arginine effectively decreases energy dissipation in water by preventing direct contact with water molecules. Therefore, d-Arg@CDs exhibit improved NIR emission in water.

The cytotoxicity of CDs and d-Arg@CDs was evaluated in HepG2, Huh7, and Hepa1-6 cells, revealing low levels of cytotoxicity for CDs and d-Arg@CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a, CDs and d-Arg@CDs had a minimal effect on cell viability at 100 μg/mL. Zeta potential analysis indicated that CDs have a negative surface charge (Fig. 4b). d-arginine has a positive charge. After modification with d-arginine, the surface charge of d-Arg@CDs became more positive, which was beneficial for crossing the negatively charged cell membrane. Fig. 4c shows distinct red FL images of Hepa1-6 cells incubated with d-Arg@CDs for 1.5 h under excitation with a 543 nm laser. Conversely, CDs incubated cells exhibited significantly weaker red FL signals. Observations under high magnification (Fig. S7) showed that d-Arg@CDs were mainly distributed in the cytoplasm. Similar observations were reported in our previous study [28]. These results reveal that the reversed surface charge positively facilitated the enhanced cellular uptake of d-Arg@CDs for bioimaging and biomedical applications.

Live/dead cell imaging assays were conducted on Hepa1-6 cells to demonstrate the photothermally induced killing ability of d-Arg@CDs. The cells were treated with d-Arg@CDs or CDs (100 µg mL−1) for 1 h, irradiated with a 655-nm laser for 10 min, and then stained with calcein acetoxymethyl ester at 37℃ for 30 min. After three washes with a PBS solution, the cells were examined using an FL microscope. The findings revealed that the cells exhibited green FL before light irradiation, indicating that the cells were in live condition and further confirming the excellent biocompatibility of d-Arg@CDs. After laser irradiation, the cells exhibited red FL, indicating that the tumor cells had died (Fig. 4d and Fig. S8). These results indicate the potent tumor-killing abilities of d-Arg@CDs via a photothermal mechanism. In addition, d-Arg@CDs demonstrated efficient tumor uptake and localized PTT, enabling effective tumor eradication while minimizing toxicity to healthy tissues.

Considering the low cytotoxicity, high cellular uptake characterized by robust red FL, and excellent luminescence in the NIR region, the biocompatibility and biodistribution of CDs, d-Arg@CDs, and l-Arg@CDs were evaluated through in vivo FL imaging. Major organs, including the heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys, were excised at different time intervals (1 h, 3 h, 12 h, 24 h, and 48 h) after intravenous injection of CDs, d-Arg@CDs, and l-Arg@CDs for ex vivo FL imaging, as illustrated in Fig. 5 a. Before injection, the major organs did not show red FL under excitation by a 589 nm laser. However, 1 h after intravenous injection of d-Arg@CDs, the liver and kidneys exhibited red FL signals under 589 nm laser excitation. In contrast, for CDs and l-Arg@CDs, these signals gradually diminished and eventually disappeared 24 h after injection. Remarkably, even after 48 h, the kidney still exhibited FL for d-Arg@CDs, indicating that d-Arg-modified CDs have a prolonged metabolism time in mice. The three groups of mice received intravenous injections of CDs, d-Arg@CDs, and l-Arg@CDs aqueous solution at a dose of 200 μL (100 μg mL−1), as illustrated in Fig. 5b. Subsequently, the mice showed intense red FL throughout their bodies within the initial 1 h, which gradually diminished after 6 h. After the modification of CDs with d-Arg and l-Arg, d-Arg@CDs and l-Arg@CDs accumulate in tumor tissues. However, d-Arg@CDs remained in tumor tissues for longer periods, indicating that d-arginine extends the metabolism of CDs in vivo and increases their accumulation in tumor tissues.

The performance of d-Arg@CDs for in vivo tumor PTT in the NIR region was further evaluated via intravenous injection, followed by treatment with a 655 nm laser at 1 W cm−2. The tumor region injected with d-Arg@CDs exhibited a temperature increase of approximately 70℃ under laser irradiation for 10 min, as illustrated in Fig. 6 a. Conversely, during the same treatment Period, tumor sections injected with CDs and PBS reached temperatures of approximately 47℃ and 37℃, respectively. The PTT capability of d-Arg@CDs exceeded that of CDs, highlighting the potential of d-Arg@CDs for more effective tumor ablation. As shown in Fig. 6b and Fig. S4, the PTT efficacy of d-Arg@CDs was further evaluated by monitoring tumor growth. In the control group, tumor tissues exhibited continuous growth over time, and neither d-Arg@CDs nor laser irradiation alone effectively inhibited tumor progression. However, the CD PTT group exhibited minimal inhibition of tumor growth, primarily due to the relatively low photothermal temperature. Conversely, the group of mice treated with d-Arg@CDs exhibited significant and remarkable inhibition of tumor growth, resulting in complete ablation with no recurrence observed even after 60 days (Fig. 6b). These results highlight the superior in vivo PTT performance of d-Arg@CDs. To further assess the anti-tumor effect, histopathological and immunohistochemical analyses were performed on the tumor tissues of mice on day 14 post-treatment. The control groups did not exhibit obvious damage to tumor tissues, as illustrated in Fig. 6d. In contrast, in the d-Arg@CDs + Laser group, H&E, c-Cas-3, and Ki67 stained images indicated significant cell damage and apoptosis, accompanied by a reduction in proliferative activity. These results confirmed the therapeutic effect of PTT in tumors. No appreciable signs of organ damage or inflammatory lesions were visible in the test of the d-Arg@CDs treatment group, suggesting low systemic toxicity of d-Arg@CDs to all these major organs (Figs. S5 and S6).

In summary, CDs were successfully modified with d-arginine, leading to the formation of a novel photothermal agent known as d-Arg@CDs. The synthesized d-Arg@CDs exhibited enhanced FL efficiency in aqueous solutions while preserving their photothermal conversion effect of 55 %. In addition, by augmenting their accumulation at the tumor site, the efficacy of these CDs in tumor treatment was significantly enhanced, thereby optimizing multiple functionalities simultaneously. d-Arg@CDs are expected to serve as highly efficient photothermal agents for tumor PTT. Furthermore, this d-arginine modification strategy may be suitable to other CDs systems to promote their photothermal properties for clinical trials.

ce:section.ce:para: All animal experiments were approved by the University of Macau Animal Ethics Committee under protocol no. UMARE-048–2023.

Liming Wang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Jun Wu: Data curation, Methodology. Bingzhe Wang: Methodology. Guichuan Xing: Resources, Funding acquisition. Songnan Qu: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition, Software, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Science and Technology Development Fund of Macau SAR (0139/2022/A3, 0007/2021/AKP, 006/2022/ALC), the Shenzhen-Hong Kong-Macao Science and Technology Innovation Project (category C) (No. SGDX20210823103803021), and the University of Macau-Huafa Group Joint Laboratory (HF-001-2021).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2024.09.135.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
